title: T. rex
gross_fact: Some T. rex skulls show deep bone damage linked to infections similar to those seen in modern birds, which could eat into the face and leave swollen, hole-like wounds while the animal was still alive.

title: Tarbosaurus
gross_fact: Tarbosaurus had a bone-crushing bite strong enough to snap and strip skeletons, meaning it could break through tough bodies and leave behind cracked bones like leftovers from a brutal prehistoric meal.

title: Albertosaurus
gross_fact: Fossils of Albertosaurus are often found in groups, suggesting they may have moved together, turning the landscape into a silent pack of predators following scents, tracks, and weakness across long distances.

title: Giganotosaurus
gross_fact: Its long, serrated teeth worked like knives, slicing through flesh instead of crushing, meaning a single bite could leave deep cuts rather than clean breaks, like a walking set of sharp blades.

title: Allosaurus
gross_fact: Allosaurus constantly replaced its teeth, so even if it lost some while feeding, new ones were already growing in behind, turning its mouth into a never-ending supply of sharp, disposable weapons.

title: Ceratosaurus
gross_fact: The horn on its nose may have been used for pushing and fighting, turning its face into a battering tool that could shove rivals or prey with surprising force.

title: Carnotaurus
gross_fact: Carnotaurus had extremely tiny arms that were almost useless, but its powerful neck and fast bite made up for it, turning its head into the main weapon in a body built for speed and impact.

title: Majungasaurus
gross_fact: Some Majungasaurus bones have clear bite marks from other Majungasaurus, suggesting it sometimes fed on its own kind, leaving behind a fossil record of prehistoric cannibal behavior.

title: Dilophosaurus
gross_fact: Its double crest made its head look larger and more dramatic, possibly helping it scare rivals or attract attention, turning its face into a strange display structure as much as a hunting tool.

title: Spinosaurus
gross_fact: Spinosaurus spent much of its time in water and had jaws shaped like a crocodile’s, packed with conical teeth designed to grip slippery prey and keep it from escaping.

title: Baryonyx
gross_fact: Baryonyx had a huge curved claw on its hand that worked like a hook, allowing it to snag fish or slash at prey with a single, precise movement.

title: Suchomimus
gross_fact: Its long, narrow snout and tightly packed teeth were perfect for gripping prey, meaning once something was caught, it had very little chance of slipping free.

title: Irritator
gross_fact: The only known skull of Irritator was altered before scientists studied it, making its discovery feel like a puzzle, where even the fossil itself carries signs of human interference.

title: Deinonychus
gross_fact: Deinonychus had a large, curved claw on each foot that could slash or pin prey, acting like a hooked blade attached directly to its body.

title: Velociraptor
gross_fact: Covered in feathers, Velociraptor likely looked less like a reptile and more like a sharp-clawed bird, combining soft textures with deadly tools in a way that feels strangely familiar and unsettling.

title: Utahraptor
gross_fact: Utahraptor was much larger than other raptors, with massive claws strong enough to latch onto prey and hold it down, turning its attack into a controlled, gripping struggle.

title: Microraptor
gross_fact: Microraptor had four wings, with feathers on both its arms and legs, creating a strange gliding shape that looked more like a living trap than a normal animal.

title: Archaeopteryx
gross_fact: Archaeopteryx combines dinosaur features like teeth and claws with bird-like feathers, making it a real-life example of a creature caught between two worlds.

title: Ankylosaurus
gross_fact: Ankylosaurus had a heavy tail club that could swing with enough force to break bones, turning its back half into a weapon capable of crushing anything that came too close.

title: Euoplocephalus
gross_fact: Covered in thick armor plates, Euoplocephalus had skin reinforced with bone, creating a natural shield that made biting into it extremely difficult.

title: Borealopelta
gross_fact: One Borealopelta fossil is so well preserved that its armor and body shape remain intact, making it look like a real animal frozen in stone, as if it simply lay down and never got back up.

title: Stegosaurus
gross_fact: Stegosaurus had large plates along its back that may have been filled with blood vessels, possibly helping regulate heat or create color displays that made it look even stranger.

title: Kentrosaurus
gross_fact: Kentrosaurus carried sharp spikes across its back and tail, meaning even standing near it could be dangerous, like walking too close to a living wall of spears.

title: Triceratops
gross_fact: Fossil skulls of Triceratops often show injuries and healed wounds, suggesting they used their horns in real fights that left lasting marks on their bodies.

title: Styracosaurus
gross_fact: Its frill was lined with long spikes that formed a crown of sharp points, making its head look more like a trap than a simple body part.

title: Pachyrhinosaurus
gross_fact: Instead of long horns, Pachyrhinosaurus had a thick, bony mass on its nose, which may have been used to push, ram, or press against others in close combat.

title: Protoceratops
gross_fact: Some Protoceratops fossils are preserved in dramatic positions, as if caught in a sudden event, giving the feeling of a moment frozen in time.

title: Psittacosaurus
gross_fact: Psittacosaurus had long bristle-like structures on its tail, creating a strange mix of scales and stiff fibers that made its body look partly armored, partly hairy.

title: Parasaurolophus
gross_fact: Its long, hollow crest may have worked like a sound chamber, allowing it to produce deep, echoing calls that could travel far across its environment.

title: Corythosaurus
gross_fact: The helmet-shaped crest on Corythosaurus likely amplified sound, turning its head into a natural instrument capable of producing strange, resonant noises.

title: Edmontosaurus
gross_fact: Some Edmontosaurus fossils preserve detailed skin impressions, revealing a thick, pebbly texture that shows dinosaurs were far from smooth or simple-looking.

title: Iguanodon
gross_fact: Iguanodon had a sharp thumb spike that could be used as a weapon, turning an otherwise plant-eating dinosaur into something with a built-in stabbing tool.

title: Ouranosaurus
gross_fact: Its tall back sail gave it a strange silhouette, like a land animal carrying a vertical ridge that made it look larger and more unusual than its body alone.

title: Gallimimus
gross_fact: Gallimimus had large eyes and long legs built for speed, allowing it to react quickly and disappear across open ground before danger could reach it.

title: Oviraptor
gross_fact: Fossils show Oviraptor positioned over nests, suggesting it may have protected its eggs, sitting quietly while ready to defend them with claws and beak.

title: Anzu
gross_fact: Anzu was a tall, feathered dinosaur with a beak and long limbs, giving it a bird-like appearance that feels familiar at first and unsettling once you notice its size.

title: Gigantoraptor
gross_fact: Gigantoraptor combined a bird-like body with enormous size, and its eggs were large enough to feel more like stone objects than fragile shells.

title: Therizinosaurus
gross_fact: Therizinosaurus had extremely long claws on its hands, so large and curved that they looked more like tools than body parts.

title: Deinocheirus
gross_fact: Deinocheirus had a mix of features including a broad snout, long arms, and a hump on its back, creating a shape that feels assembled from mismatched parts.

title: Compsognathus
gross_fact: Compsognathus was small and quick, with sharp teeth that made it an efficient hunter despite its size, like a tiny predator moving silently through its surroundings.

title: Sinosauropteryx
gross_fact: Sinosauropteryx had a covering of simple feathers, giving it a fuzzy appearance that makes it look closer to a mammal than a reptile.

title: Yi qi
gross_fact: Yi qi had unusual wing membranes stretched between elongated fingers, making it look less like a typical dinosaur and more like something experimenting with flight.

title: Kulindadromeus
gross_fact: Kulindadromeus showed both scales and feather-like structures, proving some dinosaurs had mixed body coverings that do not match simple categories.

title: Pachycephalosaurus
gross_fact: Its thick, rounded skull dome may have been used for head-butting, meaning its head could absorb repeated impacts during confrontations.

title: Stygimoloch
gross_fact: Stygimoloch had a skull covered in spikes and bumps, giving its head a rough, uneven surface that looked more like a weapon than a face.

title: Diplodocus
gross_fact: Diplodocus had a long, whip-like tail that could move quickly, potentially creating a sharp cracking motion through the air.

title: Apatosaurus
gross_fact: Each step of Apatosaurus could press deeply into the ground, leaving behind massive footprints that changed the surface of the landscape.

title: Brachiosaurus
gross_fact: Brachiosaurus reached high into the trees with its long neck, feeding far above other animals and giving it a towering presence in its environment.

title: Giraffatitan
gross_fact: Giraffatitan stood extremely tall, with a body built to support its height, making it one of the most physically imposing dinosaurs known.

title: Mamenchisaurus
gross_fact: Mamenchisaurus had an extremely long neck, making its body appear stretched out in a way that feels unusual even among large dinosaurs.

title: Argentinosaurus
gross_fact: Argentinosaurus reached enormous sizes, with limbs so large they resembled pillars supporting a moving structure rather than a typical animal body.

title: Patagotitan
gross_fact: Patagotitan was so large that its movement likely affected the ground beneath it, with each step carrying significant weight across the terrain.

title: Saltasaurus
gross_fact: Saltasaurus had small bony plates embedded in its skin, adding a layer of protection that made even a long-necked dinosaur harder to attack.

title: Shunosaurus
gross_fact: Shunosaurus had a tail club similar to armored dinosaurs, meaning even a plant-eater could swing a solid weapon behind it.

title: Amargasaurus
gross_fact: Amargasaurus had tall spines along its neck that formed a striking outline, making its shape stand out sharply against its surroundings.

title: Nigersaurus
gross_fact: Nigersaurus had hundreds of teeth arranged in rows, constantly replacing them, creating a mouth that functioned like a continuous cutting surface.

title: Dreadnoughtus
gross_fact: Dreadnoughtus was built on a massive scale, with a body so large that it likely had few natural threats once fully grown.

title: Plateosaurus
gross_fact: Many Plateosaurus fossils are found together in positions that suggest they became trapped in mud, unable to escape once they stepped into soft ground.

title: Coelophysis
gross_fact: Some Coelophysis fossils have been interpreted as showing remains of smaller individuals inside them, suggesting they may have fed on their own species.

title: Tooth Conveyor
gross_fact: Dinosaurs constantly replaced their teeth, with new ones forming beneath and behind old ones, creating a system where sharp teeth were always ready.

title: Bite Scars
gross_fact: Fossil bones often show bite marks that later healed, proving dinosaurs survived attacks and carried those marks for the rest of their lives.

title: Poop Fossil
gross_fact: Dinosaur droppings fossilized into coprolites, and some contain bits of bone or scales, preserving what they ate in surprising detail.

title: Vomit Rock
gross_fact: Regurgitated material could fossilize, leaving behind compact piles of partially digested remains that act like snapshots of a meal gone wrong.

title: Stone Stomachs
gross_fact: Some dinosaurs swallowed stones that stayed inside their bodies, helping grind food as it moved through the digestive system.

title: Egg Pores
gross_fact: Dinosaur eggshells had tiny pores that allowed gases to pass through, helping developing embryos breathe while still protected inside.

title: Nest Cities
gross_fact: Some nesting sites held large numbers of eggs and individuals, creating dense areas where many dinosaurs gathered at once.

title: Amber Tick
gross_fact: Ticks preserved in amber show that dinosaurs likely dealt with parasites, just like modern animals, carrying tiny hitchhikers on their bodies.

title: Feather Fuzz
gross_fact: Many dinosaurs had feathers or fuzzy coverings, meaning their appearance could be softer and more complex than the classic scaly image.

title: UV Patterns
gross_fact: If dinosaurs could see ultraviolet light, their bodies may have had patterns invisible to human eyes, used for signaling or display.

title: Air Sacs
gross_fact: Bird-like air sacs may have filled parts of dinosaur bodies, creating a system that extended beyond simple lungs and affected how they breathed.

title: Hollow Bones
gross_fact: Some dinosaur bones were hollow inside with internal structures, making them strong while reducing weight.

title: Trackway Chase
gross_fact: Sets of footprints found together can show movement patterns, sometimes suggesting one animal followed or moved quickly behind another.

title: Mud Trap
gross_fact: Fossil trackways sometimes show slipping and dragging marks, suggesting animals struggled in soft ground before escaping or becoming stuck.

title: Bonebed Hill
gross_fact: Some fossil sites contain many individuals together, forming bonebeds that preserve entire groups in one location.

title: Ash Blanket
gross_fact: Volcanic ash can cover large areas quickly, preserving animals and plants beneath layers that harden into rock over time.

title: Flood Pile
gross_fact: Water movement can carry and gather bones into concentrated areas, forming deposits that mix remains from different individuals.

title: Dino Burrows
gross_fact: Some dinosaurs likely created underground burrows, using them as shelter or nesting spaces hidden below the surface.

title: Egg Guardians
gross_fact: Fossil evidence suggests some dinosaurs remained with their eggs, guarding them and possibly providing warmth or protection until they hatched.

title: Dino Breath
gross_fact: Meat-eating dinosaurs likely had strong odors from food trapped between teeth and natural bacteria in their mouths.

title: Extinction Night
gross_fact: A global disaster ended the age of dinosaurs, changing the planet rapidly and affecting life on a massive scale.
